title: Purpose as a Practice, Not a Destination
subtitle: Why searching for your purpose tends not to work, and what does
excerpt: A reading about the destination model of purpose: why the idea of a fixed purpose to be found tends to produce paralysis rather than clarity. And what the alternative model, purpose as a practice built through action, actually looks like.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on the psychological and philosophical literature on meaning, motivation, and self-determination.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the search for purpose feels stuck, or when the idea of having a clear single purpose is producing more anxiety than direction.

content.text: The culture around purpose has produced a particular model: purpose as a single, specific thing that is yours to find. A calling. A mission. Something that, once located, organises everything else and makes the life make sense. This model is compelling. It is also, for most people, not how purpose actually works.
The problem with the destination model

The destination model of purpose, in which there is a single right answer waiting to be discovered, tends to produce two problems. The first is paralysis: if there is one correct purpose and you have not found it, any action in a direction that might be wrong feels like a risk. The result is staying in place, waiting for certainty before beginning.

The second problem is the imposter effect: if you think you have found your purpose but then feel doubtful or unmotivated, the model interprets this as evidence that you were wrong. Purpose, in this model, is supposed to sustain itself. Any difficulty with it becomes a sign that the search should continue.
Neither of these problems is inherent to purpose itself. They are artefacts of the destination model.

The research on meaning and purpose suggests that purpose is more reliably built through action than discovered through reflection. People who report high levels of purpose tend to have found it by doing things that mattered to them, noticing the doing mattered, and continuing. The clarity came after the action, not before it.

This inverts the search. Instead of: figure out your purpose, then act, the practice model says: act in the direction of what pulls you, notice what produces meaning, keep going in that direction. Purpose emerges from the pattern of what you keep returning to, not from a revelation that precedes it.
Small purposes and the big one

Most people who report a strong sense of purpose have not identified one singular thing. They have multiple threads: the relationship work, the creative work, the contribution to a community or cause, the daily care of someone. These threads together constitute the lived sense of purpose. The single defining mission is a rarer thing than the culture around purpose suggests.

Small purposes count. The purpose in being a reliable friend. The purpose in doing a job well. The purpose in raising a child. The purpose in making something that did not exist before. These are not lesser versions of a real purpose. For most people, they are the actual substance of one.

Purpose shifts across a life. What mattered deeply at 30 may feel less central at 50. A purpose built around parenthood changes when children become adults. A purpose built around a job changes when the job ends. The destination model sees this as failure: if the purpose was real, why has it changed?

The practice model sees it differently: purpose is responsive to where you are in your life, what you have available to give, and what the current context needs. Shifts in purpose are a normal feature of a life lived with attention. They are not signs that the previous purpose was wrong.

If the destination model has not been working, the practice model begins with a different question. Not: what is my purpose? But: what do I keep being drawn toward? What do I find myself caring about even when I am trying not to? What would I do if no one was watching? The answer to those questions is closer to the actual direction than any answer produced by searching for a fixed purpose.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on research into meaning, purpose, and what makes work feel worthwhile.
